advice: For fever, take rest, drink clean water and fluids. Place a cool, damp cloth on the forehead. If the fever is above 102°F or lasts more than 2 days, visit the nearest clinic.
For headache, rest in a quiet, dark room and drink water. Avoid loud noise and bright light. If the headache is severe, frequent, or with vomiting, seek medical help.
For cold and cough, drink warm fluids, take steam inhalation, and rest well. If cough lasts more than 1 week or has blood, consult a doctor immediately.
For mild stomach pain, drink clean water and eat light, bland food. Avoid spicy and oily items. If pain is severe, with vomiting or blood in stool, visit the nearest hospital.
For diarrhea, drink ORS or rice water to prevent dehydration. Avoid street food. If diarrhea lasts more than 2 days, or with blood, see a doctor.
For vomiting, sip small amounts of clean water or ORS. Avoid solid food until vomiting stops. If continuous or with blood, go to the hospital quickly.
For dehydration, drink ORS solution, coconut water, or lemon water with salt and sugar. Rest in a cool place. If very weak or unconscious, seek immediate medical care.
For minor cuts, wash the wound with clean water, apply antiseptic, and cover with a clean cloth. If bleeding does not stop or wound is deep, go to the nearest clinic.
Keep the person calm and still. Do not cut, suck, or apply herbs to the bite. Tie a cloth above the bite (not too tight). Take the patient to the hospital immediately.
Wash the bite with soap and water for 15 minutes. Cover with a clean cloth. Go to the hospital immediately for anti-rabies vaccine.
For small burns, hold under cool running water for 10 minutes. Do not apply toothpaste or oil. Cover with a clean cloth. For large burns, seek emergency care immediately.
For toothache, rinse with warm salt water. Avoid very hot or cold foods. If swelling or pain continues, consult a dentist.
For red or infected eye, wash with clean water. Do not rub the eye. If pain, swelling, or pus occurs, visit a doctor.
For breathing difficulty, sit upright, loosen tight clothes, and stay calm. If inhaler prescribed, use it. If breathing does not improve, call for emergency help immediately.
For pregnancy emergency or heavy bleeding, keep the mother calm and lying down. Do not give food or water. Take her to the nearest hospital immediately.

For vaginal bleeding outside normal periods, heavy bleeding, or unusual discharge, please consult a gynecologist promptly. If bleeding is very heavy (soaking a pad every hour) or accompanied by severe pain, seek immediate medical attention.

Pelvic pain in women can have various causes including ovarian cysts, infections, or other gynecological conditions. Please consult a gynecologist for proper evaluation, especially if pain is severe or persistent.

If you have missed your period and are sexually active, consider taking a pregnancy test. Morning sickness, breast tenderness, and fatigue are common early pregnancy symptoms. Consult a healthcare provider for confirmation and prenatal care.

Any breast changes including lumps, pain, or nipple discharge should be evaluated by a healthcare provider. While many breast symptoms are benign, proper medical examination is important for accurate diagnosis.
For burning during urination or frequent urination, increase water intake and consult a doctor for proper diagnosis. UTIs are common and easily treatable with antibiotics when diagnosed properly.

Chest pain can be serious. If you have severe chest pain, pain radiating to arm/jaw, sweating, or shortness of breath, call emergency services immediately. For mild chest discomfort, rest and avoid physical activity until evaluated by a doctor.

For high blood pressure, reduce salt intake, maintain healthy weight, exercise regularly, and avoid stress. Monitor BP regularly. If BP is very high (>180/110) with symptoms like headache or dizziness, seek immediate medical care.
For heart palpitations, sit down, take deep breaths, and stay calm. Avoid caffeine and alcohol. If palpitations are severe, persistent, or with chest pain/dizziness, seek immediate medical attention.

For shortness of breath, sit upright, loosen tight clothing, and remain calm. If severe, sudden, or with chest pain, call emergency services immediately. Chronic breathlessness requires medical evaluation.

For symptoms suggestive of pneumonia (fever, cough with phlegm, chest pain), rest, stay hydrated, and see a doctor promptly for evaluation and possible antibiotics. Elderly and immunocompromised should seek immediate care.

For wheezing or asthma symptoms, use prescribed inhaler if available, sit upright, and stay calm. If severe wheezing, unable to speak in full sentences, or no improvement with medication, seek emergency care immediately.
STROKE EMERGENCY: If someone has face drooping, arm weakness, or speech problems, call emergency services IMMEDIATELY. Time is critical for stroke treatment. Do not give food, water, or medications.

During a seizure: Keep person safe, don't restrain, place something soft under head, turn to side after seizure stops. Call emergency if seizure lasts >5 minutes, person is injured, or has difficulty breathing after.

For dizziness, sit down immediately, stay hydrated, and avoid sudden movements. If dizziness is severe, persistent, or with hearing loss/headache, consult a doctor. Sudden severe dizziness may require emergency care.

For memory problems or confusion, ensure safety first. If sudden confusion or severe memory loss, seek immediate medical evaluation. Gradual memory changes should be discussed with a healthcare provider for proper assessment.

For acid reflux, eat smaller meals, avoid spicy/fatty foods, don't lie down after eating, and elevate head while sleeping. If symptoms persist, worsen, or include difficulty swallowing, consult a doctor.

For constipation, increase fiber intake, drink more water, exercise regularly, and establish regular bathroom routine. If severe abdominal pain, vomiting, or no bowel movement for >3 days, see a doctor.
Blood in stool requires immediate medical attention. Bright red blood or black tarry stools can indicate serious conditions. Do not ignore - consult a doctor or go to emergency room immediately.
Yellow coloring of eyes or skin suggests liver problems requiring immediate medical attention. Avoid alcohol, rest, and see a doctor urgently. This can be serious and needs proper evaluation.
For back pain, rest in comfortable position, apply heat/ice, gentle stretching, and avoid heavy lifting. If pain radiates to legs, causes numbness, or is severe after injury, see a doctor promptly.

For joint pain, rest affected joint, apply ice for acute pain or heat for stiffness, gentle movement, and avoid overuse. If joints are red, hot, swollen, or severely limiting movement, consult a doctor.
For muscle cramps, gently stretch and massage the muscle, apply heat, stay hydrated, and ensure adequate electrolytes. If cramps are frequent, severe, or don't resolve, consult a healthcare provider.

For suspected fracture, don't move the injured area, apply ice, elevate if possible, and seek immediate medical care. Do not attempt to realign or move the bone. Call emergency services for severe injuries.

For skin rash, keep area clean and dry, avoid scratching, use mild soap, and identify/avoid triggers. If rash spreads rapidly, has blisters, or is accompanied by fever/breathing problems, seek immediate medical care.

For eczema, moisturize regularly, use mild fragrance-free products, avoid known triggers, and keep fingernails short. If severe flare-up, infection signs, or not improving with care, consult a dermatologist.

For acne, wash face gently twice daily, don't pick or squeeze, use non-comedogenic products, and maintain good hygiene. If severe, cystic, or causing scarring, consult a dermatologist for treatment options.

For diabetes symptoms (frequent urination, excessive thirst, fatigue), monitor blood sugar if possible, maintain healthy diet, and consult a doctor for proper testing. Extremely high blood sugar requires immediate medical attention.

Thyroid symptoms (weight changes, fatigue, heart rate changes, mood changes) require medical evaluation and blood tests. Take prescribed medications as directed and follow up regularly with healthcare provider.

For depression or suicidal thoughts, reach out to mental health professionals, trusted friends/family, or crisis helplines immediately. You're not alone - help is available. In crisis, call emergency services or go to nearest hospital.

For anxiety, practice deep breathing, grounding techniques, regular exercise, and adequate sleep. If anxiety significantly impacts daily life or includes panic attacks, consult a mental health professional for support and treatment options.

For sleep problems, maintain regular sleep schedule, avoid caffeine/screens before bed, create comfortable sleep environment, and practice relaxation techniques. If chronic insomnia persists, consult a doctor to rule out underlying conditions.

For suspected malaria, typhoid, or dengue (high fever, chills, headache, body aches), seek immediate medical testing and treatment. These conditions can be serious if untreated. Maintain hydration and rest while awaiting medical care.

For TB symptoms (persistent cough >2 weeks, weight loss, night sweats, fatigue), get medical evaluation and testing immediately. TB is treatable but requires proper medical management and completion of full antibiotic course.
For food poisoning, stay hydrated with clear fluids, rest, and eat bland foods when able. If severe dehydration, high fever, blood in vomit/stool, or symptoms worsen, seek medical attention promptly.

POISON EMERGENCY: Call poison control center or emergency services immediately. Do not induce vomiting unless instructed. If conscious, give water/milk only if advised. Bring the substance container if safe to do so.

SEVERE ALLERGY EMERGENCY: Call emergency services immediately if there's swelling of face/throat, difficulty breathing, rapid pulse, or dizziness after exposure to allergen. Use epinephrine auto-injector if available.

CHOKING EMERGENCY: For choking adult - encourage coughing, if ineffective perform Heimlich maneuver (abdominal thrusts). For infant - back blows and chest thrusts. Call emergency services if object doesn't dislodge.

For infant fever (<3 months with fever >100.4°F), seek immediate medical care. For older children, ensure hydration, comfortable clothing, and monitor closely. If fever >103°F, difficulty breathing, or child appears very ill, see doctor urgently.

For child's cough, ensure hydration, humid air, and honey for >1 year old. If baby <3 months, difficulty breathing, wheezing, or cough persists >1 week, consult pediatrician. Barking cough may indicate croup - seek medical care.

For elderly health concerns, ensure medication compliance, regular check-ups, fall prevention measures, and social support. Any sudden changes in mental status, mobility, or function require prompt medical evaluation.
